financebench_id_01328.verdict: Gold contradicts the question's own conditional — pindex's "0" is the instructed answer.
financebench_id_01328.reasoning: The question asks for restructuring costs "directly outlined in Pepsico's income statements for FY2022" and then explicitly instructs: "If restructuring costs are not explicitly outlined then state 0." Read directly from the PDF, the Consolidated Statement of Income (PDF p.62) contains no restructuring line item — its complete line list is: Net Revenue, Cost of sales, Gross profit, Selling general and administrative expenses, Gain associated with the Juice Transaction, Impairment of intangible assets, Operating Profit, Other pension and retiree medical benefits income, Net interest expense and other, Income before income taxes, Provision for income taxes, Net income. The conditional is therefore triggered and the instructed answer is 0 — which pindex returned at all four efforts, with reasoning that enumerated the income-statement lines correctly. The gold answer ($411 million) is a real figure, but it lives in Note 3 — Restructuring and Impairment Charges (PDF p.78, "Total restructuring and impairment charges $411"), i.e. NOT directly outlined on the income statement. The gold answer ignores the question's own escape clause, so this is a benchmark error: the model is right and the gold is wrong for the question as written. This also answers "why did we see 0": it was not a retrieval or reasoning failure — 0 is the output the question commands in exactly this situation; the judge marked it wrong only because it compared against the (mistaken) gold.
evidence: Question text: "…directly outlined in Pepsico's income statements for FY2022? If restructuring costs are not explicitly outlined then state 0."

PDF p.62, Consolidated Statement of Income — full line list has no restructuring item (Net Revenue / Cost of sales / Gross profit / SG&A / Gain associated with the Juice Transaction / Impairment of intangible assets / Operating Profit / …).
PDF p.78, Note 3 — Restructuring and Impairment Charges: "Total restructuring and impairment charges $411" (2022), the source of the gold figure — a note, not the income statement.
financebench_id_01328.suggested_reason: The question's own conditional ("If restructuring costs are not explicitly outlined then state 0") is triggered: the Consolidated Statement of Income (PDF p.62) carries no restructuring line item — verified directly against the PDF, whose income-statement lines are exactly those pindex enumerated. Gold's $411M is real but comes from Note 3 (PDF p.78, "Total restructuring and impairment charges $411"), which is not "directly outlined in the income statement". The gold answer ignores the question's escape clause; pindex answered the question as written at every effort.

financebench_id_03620.verdict: Gold verified correct from the PDF; low/medium made a genuine sign/line-item error (15,555 vs 9,068).
financebench_id_03620.reasoning: Gold ($9,068M) is exactly computable from the two statements the question permits: Operating Profit $11,512M (income statement, PDF p.62) + Depreciation and amortization $2,763M (cash flow, PDF p.64) − Capital spending $5,207M (cash flow, PDF p.64) = $9,068M. high/ultra produced exactly this and were judged AL. low/medium over-interpreted "unadjusted": instead of taking Operating Profit as reported, they 'un-adjusted' it by adding back the impairment of intangibles (+$3,166M) and the Juice Transaction gain with its sign flipped (+$3,321M instead of −$3,321M): 11,512 + 3,321 + 3,166 + 2,763 − 5,207 = 15,555. That is both a misreading of "unadjusted" (the as-reported figure needs no adjustment) and a sign error on the gain. A genuine miss — NAL stands for low/medium.
evidence: PDF p.62: Operating Profit 11,512 (2022).
PDF p.64: Depreciation and amortization 2,763; Capital spending (5,207).
11,512 + 2,763 − 5,207 = 9,068 = gold; high/ultra answered $9,068 million (AL).
low/medium reasoning adjusted Operating Profit for the Juice gain and impairment: 11,512 + 3,321 + 3,166 + 2,763 − 5,207 = 15,555.

financebench_id_00476.verdict: Confirmed genuine miss — the answer is on the cover page pindex never visited.
financebench_id_00476.reasoning: The Amex 2022 10-K cover page's Section 12(b) table lists only "Common Shares (par value $0.20 per Share), AXP, New York Stock Exchange" and 12(g) is "None" — so gold ("There are none") is correct and answerable from page 1. pindex retrieved Note 8 (long-term debt, pp.124–127), listed outstanding notes, and claimed the filing "does not explicitly specify" exchange registration — false; the cover page does. Root cause: the tree's Preface node summary (pages 1–4) compresses the registration table to "Stock traded on NYSE under ticker AXP", so the absence-of-registered-debt fact never reaches retrieval, and the question's keywords pull the agent to the debt note instead. An absence-fact summary-lossiness case to revisit as the pool grows.
evidence: PDF p.1: "Securities registered pursuant to Section 12(b) of the Act: Common Shares (par value $0.20 per Share) | AXP | New York Stock Exchange"; "Securities registered pursuant to section 12(g) of the Act: None".
pindex (high/ultra) cited pp.124–127 (Note 8 parent-company debt) and answered that the filing doesn't specify registration status.

financebench_id_00494.verdict: Adjudicated SEDC (ultra) — same grounded facts as the AL-judged high answer; hedged framing.
financebench_id_00494.reasoning: Gold's evidence (PDF p.9) forecasts increases for 737, 777X ("expected to resume in 2023") and 787. pindex's ultra answer grounded the 737 increase and 787 return-to-5/month with accurate quotes from pp.33–35 but quoted p.34's 777X-9 pause "during 2022 and 2023" and framed guidance as limited. The high run cited the same facts framed affirmatively and was judged AL — the filing itself states both the pause and the 2023 resume, so this is an interpretive fork over the same evidence.
evidence: PDF p.9: "The 787 program is currently producing at low rates and we expect to gradually increase to 5 per month in 2023. Production of the 777X is currently paused and is expected to resume in 2023."
PDF p.34: "we decided to pause production of the 777X-9 during 2022 and 2023".

financebench_id_00216.verdict: Adjudicated SEDC (high/ultra) — computed gold's exact 0.54, then took the question's invited fork.
financebench_id_00216.reasoning: pindex computed the identical quick ratio (0.54 = ($2,605M cash + $24,506M receivables) / $50,171M current liabilities) from the correct balance sheet, then took the path the question explicitly invites ("If the quick ratio is not relevant to measure liquidity, please state that and explain why"), arguing operating cash flow is Verizon's primary liquidity source. Gold takes the other fork (0.54 → not healthy). low/medium reported the same 0.54 with gold's conclusion and were judged AL.
evidence: Same arithmetic in both gold and pindex: quick ratio ≈ 0.54.
Question text explicitly permits the not-relevant path.

financebench_id_00585.verdict: Adjudicated MVA (all efforts) — pindex reports the 10-K's own reconciliation rates; gold flips signs.
financebench_id_00585.reasoning: Boeing's tax reconciliation (PDF p.77) ends "Income tax expense/(benefit) $31 / (0.6)% … ($743) / 14.7%" — the company itself reports FY2022 (0.6)% and FY2021 14.7%, exactly what pindex answered. Gold (0.62% vs −14.76%) computes the same ratios with a normalized positive-loss denominator, flipping both signs. Magnitudes agree to rounding; high/ultra additionally gave the dollar figures and the valuation-allowance driver.
evidence: Gold: "Effective tax rate in FY2022 was 0.62%, compared to -14.76% in FY2021."

financebench_id_00790.verdict: Same verified statements, opposite verdicts on an interpretive question — gold's own caveats half-support pindex.
financebench_id_00790.reasoning: "Is CVS Health a capital-intensive business?" has no computational answer — it is a judgment call, and both sides ground theirs in accurate figures (all verified directly from the PDF). Gold says yes via the asset base: total assets $228,275M against net income $4,149M gives the 1.82% ROA gold quotes. pindex says no via capital spending: purchases of property and equipment were $2,727M against ~$322.5B revenue (~0.85%), with PP&E net of $12,873M just 5.6% of total assets and goodwill at $78,150M (34%) dominating the balance sheet. Notably gold itself concedes both of pindex's strongest points — "a significant portion of this asset base is goodwill, and CVS's fixed assets/total assets ratio is on the lower side of 5.6%" — i.e. the asset heaviness comes from acquisition intangibles, not productive fixed capital. pindex held the same conclusion with consistent grounding at all four efforts. Same evidence, defensibly different conclusion: SEDC.
evidence: PDF balance sheet: Total assets $228,275M; Goodwill $78,150M; Property and equipment, net $12,873M (5.6% of assets).
Net income attributable to CVS Health $4,149M -> ROA 1.82% (gold's figure, verified).
Cash flow: Purchases of property and equipment $(2,727)M -> ~0.85% of $322.5B revenue (pindex's figure, verified).
Gold's own caveat: "a significant portion of this asset base is goodwill, and CVS's fixed assets/total assets ratio is on the lower side of 5.6%".
financebench_id_00790.suggested_reason: Interpretive question with no computational answer; both conclusions are grounded in accurate, PDF-verified figures. Gold argues capital intensity from the $228.3B asset base / 1.82% ROA; pindex argues the opposite from capex of $2,727M (~0.85% of revenue) and PP&E at just 5.6% of total assets — and gold's own caveats (goodwill-dominated asset base, low fixed-asset ratio) concede exactly those points. Same evidence, defensibly different conclusion at all four efforts.

financebench_id_00222.verdict: Two standard quick-ratio formulas, both PDF-verified, same conclusion — adjudicated MVA stands.
financebench_id_00222.reasoning: Both computations check out exactly against AMD's Consolidated Balance Sheet (PDF p.56). Gold's formula — (cash and equivalents $4,835M + short-term investments $1,020M + accounts receivable, net $4,126M + receivables from related parties $2M) / total current liabilities $6,369M — gives 9,983/6,369 = 1.57. pindex's equally standard variant — (total current assets $15,019M − inventories $3,771M) / $6,369M — gives 11,248/6,369 = 1.77. The 0.20 gap is entirely the formula's treatment of prepaid/other current assets, not an extraction or arithmetic error, and both land on the same verdict the question actually asks for: yes, AMD's liquidity is healthy (ratio comfortably above 1). Textbook multiple-valid-approaches.
evidence: PDF p.56: Cash and cash equivalents $4,835M; Short-term investments $1,020M; Accounts receivable, net $4,126M; Receivables from related parties $2M; Total current assets $15,019M; Inventories $3,771M (implied); Total current liabilities $6,369M.
Both answers conclude AMD's liquidity profile is healthy.

financebench_id_00460.verdict: Both counts sit in the same PDF table; the question asks about "Best Buy stores" and pindex used exactly that row — adjudicated BE stands.
financebench_id_00460.reasoning: The Domestic-segment store table (PDF p.17) carries both numbers side by side. The "Best Buy" row reads 931→930 for Q2 FY2023 and 908→907 for Q2 FY2024 — pindex's 930→907 (−23 stores) is that row, read correctly. Gold's 982→969 is the "Total" row, which adds Outlet Centers (and pilot formats) to the Best Buy-branded count. The question asks for the change in "the number of Best Buy stores"; the row literally named "Best Buy" is the most faithful reading, and the same page's prose even distinguishes the concepts ("close a total of 20 to 30 Best Buy stores and open approximately 5 Outlet Centers"). Both answers agree on the direction (a decline). Gold answering from the broader Total row makes this a benchmark-error excusal rather than a model miss.
evidence: PDF p.17 Domestic store table — Best Buy row: 931→930 (Q2 FY2023), 908→907 (Q2 FY2024); Total row: 982 (Q2 FY2023 end) → 969 (Q2 FY2024 end).
Same page prose: "close a total of 20 to 30 Best Buy stores and open approximately 5 Outlet Centers in fiscal 2024" — the filing itself treats Best Buy stores and Outlet Centers as distinct.
Both answers conclude the store count declined.

financebench_id_01902.verdict: "Best by top line" — pindex answered with the largest revenue category; gold answered with the fastest-growing one — adjudicated BE stands (SEDC also defensible).
financebench_id_01902.reasoning: Both readings are PDF-verified. The revenue-by-category table (PDF p.13) shows Computing and Mobile Phones as the largest domestic category by revenue in Q2 FY2024 ($3,674M for the quarter) — pindex's answer. The MD&A category notes (PDF p.18) state "Entertainment: The 9.0% comparable sales growth was driven primarily by gaming" — gold's answer. "Top line" means revenue; "performed the best by top line" is ambiguous between the biggest top-line contributor (pindex) and the best top-line growth (gold). Gold's own phrasing ("experienced the highest growth") answers a growth question, not the revenue-magnitude question as worded — hence the historical BE call; SEDC would also be defensible since both conclusions come from the same filing with accurate figures.
evidence: PDF p.13 revenue-by-category table: Computing and Mobile Phones is the largest domestic category (quarter revenue $3,674M; six-month $8,249M).
PDF p.18: "Entertainment: The 9.0% comparable sales growth was driven primarily by gaming, partially offset by comparable sales declines in virtual reality and drones."
Question wording: "Which… product category performed the best (by top line)…" — gold answers growth, pindex answers revenue magnitude.

financebench_id_00839.verdict: Identical facts from the same 8-K; the split is purely whether Ulta Beauty counts as "similar" to Foot Locker — adjudicated SEDC stands.
financebench_id_00839.reasoning: The 8-K (PDF p.2) states Mary N. Dillon was "former Executive Chair and Chief Executive Officer of Ulta Beauty, Inc." (CEO July 2013–June 2021) and before that "President and Chief Executive Officer… of United States Cellular Corporation" (2010–2013). Both gold and pindex cite exactly these facts. Gold reasons Ulta is similar because it is a large brick-and-mortar-plus-online specialty retailer like Foot Locker; pindex reasons it is not similar because beauty retail differs from athletic footwear, while acknowledging her retail-CEO background. Neither answer misreads the document — the entire disagreement is the subjective threshold for "similar company". Same evidence, different defensible conclusion.
evidence: PDF p.2: "Mary N. Dillon, 61, former Executive Chair and Chief Executive Officer of Ulta Beauty, Inc., has been appointed President and Chief Executive Officer"; "She previously served as Chief Executive Officer… of Ulta from July 2013 through June 2021"; "President and Chief Executive Officer… of United States Cellular Corporation from 2010 through 2013".
Gold: Ulta = large retail, brick-and-mortar + online → similar. pindex: beauty vs specialty athletic retail → not similar; same underlying facts.
